Webpack 5 — Starting From Scratch (01) — Take Two
Heads up — this is actually the second time I'm writing Webpack notes. The reason for the rewrite is that the first attempt wasn't well-organised or detailed enough, so I'm starting over.
As lots of articles online point out, Webpack is a bundling tool. What's a bundling tool, exactly? Here's

Webpack is a free and open-source module bundler for JavaScript. It is made primarily for JavaScript, but it can transform front-end assets such as HTML, CSS, and images if the corresponding loaders are included.
Webpack takes modules with dependencies and generates static assets representing those modules.
OK so if you read that and went "huh?", this rewrite was worth doing.
Before I explain Webpack in plain terms, you need to know one basic fact:
browsers only understand plain CSS, HTML, JavaScript, and images
. What does that mean? In modern frontend dev, we use Sass/SCSS, Pug/Jade, EJS, CoffeeScript, and other preprocessors.
So Webpack's job is to take all of that and package it into the CSS, HTML, JavaScript, and images browsers actually understand — exactly like the
Now let's start learning Webpack and setting one up.
You'll need Node.js to run Webpack. This series sets up Webpack 5 from scratch, so install Node.js — at least version 10. If you're not sure what version you have, run:
language-cmd shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
If you haven't installed Node yet, I'd recommend installing nvm. Here are some related posts:
Install nvm on Windows 10 and switch Node.js versions (article in Mandarin)
Install nvm on macOS (article in Mandarin)
